---
title: "Data Handling"
---

Once data has been successfully imported and explored, the next step is preparation for analysis. This stage bridges raw information and meaningful results by aligning the dataset with the specific requirements of the research design. Although the exact procedures depend on the research question, countries involved, subpopulations of interest, and empirical methods, common tasks typically include transforming variables to improve comparability, subsetting data to focus on relevant groups, and constructing new variables from existing ones.

Developing familiarity with these operations is essential, as they allow researchers to adapt datasets to their analytical goals while maintaining clarity and consistency. A structured approach to data manipulation also enhances workflow efficiency and reduces the risk of errors or misinterpretation during analysis.


As in the previous section, this part of the guide is organized by variable type, distinguishing between categorical and continuous data. By the end of this section, users will be equipped with a set of practical tools and techniques that add an additional layer of flexibility to their work with LIS data, ultimately expanding the range and depth of analyses they can perform. 


## Recoding Categories {#dh_cat_vars}

The most common operation that users do with categorical variables, usally is, once familiar with the numric codes and their respective labels, to re-harmmonizing them
further in order to align them with the preferd breakdown for their multiplicity of reasson users could have, whtever whwterver. 





There is however, as mentioned earlier, the [nested coding](/r-users/dd_catg_vars.html#nested-coding) feature mentioned earlier on, that despite delivering the most about of infprmatin possible to user, it coudl also create unnexpected breaks in the series. In the charts below we see this in practice, as of 2004 onwards, LIS was delivered richer, more disaggregated data, to fill in the 'own' variables for the Spanish series. 


###  Highest-Level Recoding


![](/images/ES00_data_handling.png){fig-align="center"}
![](/images/ES04_data_handling.png){fig-align="center"}



For us to increase the comparability throught the entire series then we need to recode  the variables in the years we have more information, to the broader categories we inititially had the from the begining of the series. 

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold', message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
library(dplyr)
library(purrr)
library(labelled)

recoded_data = lissyuse('es', 'own', from =  2000, to = 2004, lws = FALSE) %>%
  purrr::map(
    ~ .x %>% mutate(
      own_copy_old  = own, # create a copy of the original variable 'own' 
      own = case_when( # do the transformations on the variable with the original name from LIS
        own >= 100 & own < 199 ~ 100, 
        own >= 210 & own >=210 & own < 219 ~ 210, 
        own >= 220 & own >=220 & own < 229 ~ 220, 
      ),
      own = haven::labelled(own, labels = labelled::val_labels(own_copy_old))
    ) 
  )

lissyrtools::run_weighted_count(data = recoded_data, var_name = 'own', na.rm = TRUE)
```


::: {.callout-important}
Functions in `lissyrtools` generally only accept columns named after original LIS or LWS variable name. No need to go in details why, but its because of the way LISSY is configured, and to avoid that some jobs go into the review queue by mistake and whtever .... Therefore, variables named like 'orig_new', or 'own_recoded would not have been accepted'. Needs to be 'orig', 'educ' 'educlev' etc ...
:::
 




![](/images/ES_ts_no_recoding.png){width=100%}

![](/images/ES_ts_recoding.png){width=100%}
